coredata.dc:title: An adequate avenue towards well-designed PBDT-DTNT:PCBM active layers via quantum dot/conductive polymer configurations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chenxi
Agbolaghi, Samira
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) and carbon quantum dots (CQD) were employed to develop the patterned GQD/CQD/poly[benzodithiophene-bis(decyltetradecyl-thien) naphthothiadiazole] (PBDT-DTNT) short/long chain, crab-like GQD/semicrab-like CQD/poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) short chain, and pellet-like GQD/CQD/P3HT long chain supramolecules. Nanostructures were used in roles of modifier, donor, and acceptor constituents in ternary (PBDT-DTNT:[6,6]-phenyl-C71 butyric acid methyl ester (PC71BM)) and binary photoactive layers. Application of pre-designed patterned quantum dot (QD)/PBDT-DTNT short chain nano-hybrids as morphology modifier promoted the performance up to 4.79%. P3HT-based supramolecules acted conspicuously better than PBDT-DTNT ones. The highest efficacies of 7.14 and 6.24% were detected in PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain (14.63 mA cm–2, 68.71%, 0.71 V and 287 Ω cm2) and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT short chain (13.35 mA cm–2, 66.79%, 0.70 V and 447 Ω cm2) systems, respectively. In addition to ternary photovoltaics, the binary devices were fabricated using the QDs and their associated supramolecules as donor and acceptor agents. Exactly similar to ternary cells, the crab/semicrab-like and pellet-like nano-hybrids, acted better than the PBDT-DTNT patterned nano-hybrids. The best results were recorded for PBDT-DTNT:GQD/P3HT short chain (2.93%) and PBDT-DTNT:CQD/P3HT short chain (2.67%) solar cells. In second type of binary photovoltaics, the QDs and their correlated supramolecules were utilized as electron donor and a maximum performance of 0.22% was acquired.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Conjugated polymer
Supramolecule
Solar cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (top left), SAED pattern (top right), lateral size histogram (bottom left) and thickness histogram (bottom right) of GQDs (a) and CQDs (b).

TEM image (top left), SAED pattern (top right), lateral size histogram (bottom left) and thickness histogram (bottom right) of patterned GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (a), patterned GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (b), patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (c) and patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (d) nanostructures.
Schemes of patterned GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (a), patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (b), patterned GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (c) and patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (d) nanostructures.

TEM image (top left), SAED patterns (top right), lateral size histogram (bottom left) and thickness histogram (bottom right) of crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (a), pellet-like GQD/P3HT long chain (b), semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain (c) and pellet-like CQD/P3HT long chain (d) nanostructures.

Schemes of crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (a), semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain nanostructures (b), pellet-like GQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures (c) and pellet-like CQD/P3HT long chain (d) nanostructures.
Configuration of a typical photovoltaic cell and the nanostructures separately applied in the active layers (a); energy levels of various nanostructures used in the photovoltaics (b).

J–V curves of ternary and binary photovoltaics based on the PBDT-DTNT and PC71BM and modified by the CQD, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short/long chain, CQD/P3HT short/long chain, GQD, GQD/PBDT-DTNT short/long chain and GQD/P3HT short/long chain precursors.
Bar graphs of Jsc (a), FF (b), Voc (c) and PCE (d) characteristics for distinct ternary and binary solar cells.

EQE curves of ternary and binary photovoltaics based on the PBDT-DTNT and PC71BM and modified by the CQD, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short/long chain, CQD/P3HT short/long chain, GQD, GQD/PBDT-DTNT short/long chain and GQD/P3HT short/long chain precursors.

Impedance response from devices with the pristine PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM active layers and those modified by CQD, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short/long chain, CQD/P3HT short/long chain, GQD, GQD/PBDT-DTNT short/long chain and GQD/P3HT short/long chain precursors (a); the PBDT-DTNT and above mentioned modifiers (b); PC71BM and above mentioned modifiers (c).
Charge transfer resistances of all ternary and binary fabricated photovoltaic cells.

head.ce:title: An adequate avenue towards well-designed PBDT-DTNT:PCBM active layers via quantum dot/conductive polymer configurations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, P.O. BOX: 5375171379, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) and carbon quantum dots (CQD) were employed to develop the patterned GQD/CQD/poly[benzodithiophene-bis(decyltetradecyl-thien) naphthothiadiazole] (PBDT-DTNT) short/long chain, crab-like GQD/semicrab-like CQD/poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) short chain, and pellet-like GQD/CQD/P3HT long chain supramolecules. Nanostructures were used in roles of modifier, donor, and acceptor constituents in ternary (PBDT-DTNT:[6,6]-phenyl-C71 butyric acid methyl ester (PC71BM)) and binary photoactive layers. Application of pre-designed patterned quantum dot (QD)/PBDT-DTNT short chain nano-hybrids as morphology modifier promoted the performance up to 4.79%. P3HT-based supramolecules acted conspicuously better than PBDT-DTNT ones. The highest efficacies of 7.14 and 6.24% were detected in PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain (14.63 mA cm–2, 68.71%, 0.71 V and 287 Ω cm2) and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT short chain (13.35 mA cm–2, 66.79%, 0.70 V and 447 Ω cm2) systems, respectively. In addition to ternary photovoltaics, the binary devices were fabricated using the QDs and their associated supramolecules as donor and acceptor agents. Exactly similar to ternary cells, the crab/semicrab-like and pellet-like nano-hybrids, acted better than the PBDT-DTNT patterned nano-hybrids. The best results were recorded for PBDT-DTNT:GQD/P3HT short chain (2.93%) and PBDT-DTNT:CQD/P3HT short chain (2.67%) solar cells. In second type of binary photovoltaics, the QDs and their correlated supramolecules were utilized as electron donor and a maximum performance of 0.22% was acquired.

ce:section.ce:para: Efficient conversion of the solar energy into either electrical or fuel sources remains one of mankind’s challenges [1,2]. Numerous researchers have focused on the elevation of performance of solar cells as a pure energy by the nanostructure/nanocomposite assistances [3–25] or through donor/acceptor design [26–30]. A notable example of polymer/nanoparticle nanocomposites is the heterojunction solar cells constructed with the conjugated polymers and quantum dots (QDs), where quantum dots act as n-type semiconductor and light absorber [31–37]. The QDs are proper for being applied in the bulk heterojunction (BHJ) solar cells because of their broad spectral range, high electron mobility, and good photo- and chemical stability [38]. In particular, the nano-phase separated mixtures of conjugated polymer/QD [39–41] have attracted interest in the photovoltaic cells [42–49]. Nanostructures on the basis of different QDs and polymer chains have been also reported in the literature [50–53].

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with lateral dimensions less than 100 nm provide several novel chemical/physical characteristics due to the quantum confinement and edge effects [54–63]. The GQDs with the moderate photoluminescence signal, non-toxicity, and cell permeability are promising in cellular imaging, biosensors, and drug delivery [64]. Li et al. [65] used the GQDs to completely replace the [6,6]-phenyl-C61 butyric acid methylester (PCBM) and obtained a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 1.28%. Gupta et al. [66] prepared the GQDs functionalized with aniline (ANI), and the as-fabricated hybrid solar cells based on the poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT):ANI-GQDs manifested a PCE of 1.14%. In another interesting work, the introduction of the GQDs into a P3HT:PCBM solar cell reflected a significant enhancement in the PCE up to 5.24% [67]. On the other hand, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm, have received extensive attention in recent years thanks to their low toxicity, good physicochemical stability, facile functionalization and fluorescence properties [68–70], which are applicable in distinct fields [71–79]. Notably, the CQDs have been also employed in different types of solar cells [80–86].

In this work, the GQD and CQD nanoparticles were employed to develop the patterned GQD/CQD/PBDT-DTNT short/long chain, crab-like GQD/semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain, and pellet-like GQD/CQD/P3HT long chain supramolecules. The designed nano-hybrids were deeply characterized and utilized in the active layers of poly[benzodithiophene-bis(decyltetradecyl-thien) naphthothiadiazole] (PBDT-DTNT):PC71BM solar cells. The nanostructures in question were used in the roles of modifier, donor, and acceptor constituents in the ternary and binary photoactive layers. The best results were detected in the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain (14.63 mA cm–2, 68.71%, 0.71 V and 7.14%) and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT short chain (13.35 mA cm–2, 66.79%, 0.70 V and 6.24%) devices.

The citric acid (2 g) and ethylenediamine (650 μL) were dissolved in deionized water and the solution reactor was switched to 250 °C for 12 h and, subsequently, it was cooled to 30 °C. The reaction solution was filtered and a dark filter solution was acquired, which was further dialyzed within 6 days and the CQDs were reached by vacuum freeze-drying [87,88].

According to the modified Hummers method [89,90], 1.0 g of graphite and 60 mL H2SO4 (98%) was stirred in an ice bath, and 5.8 g KMnO4 was added during 1 h stirring. The solution was swithched to 30 °C for 2 h, 40 mL of deionizer water was added, the reaction was heated to 90 °C for 40 min, and eventually a 80 mL of deionizer water was added. When the temperature was cooled down to 60 °C, 10 mL H2O2 (30%) and 200 mL HCl (5%) were poured and then the supernatant was decanted and centrifuged with deionizer water, and the mixture solution was further dialyzed for 2 days and the GO powder was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying. The GO precursors were then suspended in H2SO4 for further exfoliation and the solution was then filtered with the polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane and finally dialyzed to reach the GQD precursors [89].

The poly[benzodithiophene-bis(decyltetradecyl-thien) naphthothiadiazole] (PBDT-DTNT) was synthesized via stille-coupling possessing the number average molecular weights (Mn) of 60 000, 35 000, and 7500 g/mol and the dispersity ranging in 2.0–2.3 [91]. The details of preparation and characterization of PBDT-DTNT are reported in Supplementary information. The highly regioregular P3HT (<99%, Mn = 30 000 and 6000 g/mol, dispersity = 1.20–1.25) were also synthesized by Grignard metathesis polymerization. Fig. S1 reports 1HNMR spectra and SEC trace of a typical synthesized PBDT-DTNT.

The P3HT and PBDT-DTNT conjugated polymers were separately dissolved in 1,2-dichlorobenzene (1,2-DCB) at 100 °C for 45 min and then poured to the pre-dispersed QDs in the same solvent with a total concentration of 0.1 wt%. The vials were subsequently switched to the sonication (30 °C, 8 h) and crystallization (25 °C, 24 h) steps. The photoactive layer solutions were spin-coated onto the poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) modified indium tin oxide (ITO, 10–15 Ω/square) on the glass to yield 400 nm films (Tencor, Alpha-500). To complete the device fabrication, the LiF interfacial layer (1 nm) and Al electrode (100 nm) were thermally evaporated onto the device under the vacuum below 2 × 10−4 Pa. The current density–voltage (J–V) data were recorded on a computer-controlled measurement unit from Newport under the illumination of AM1.5G, 100 mW/cm2.

Herein, the graphenic and carbonic QDs were utilized to develop the supramolecular nanostructures. The diameter of GQDs ranged in 2–4 nm on the basis of transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips CM30) (Fig. 1 (a)). TEM image, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, lateral size/thickness histograms of GQDs are depicted in Fig. 1(a). According to the atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope V), the thickness of GQDs peaked at 2.8 nm in the range of 0.7–4.2 nm. The CQDs were less monodisperse, peaking at 4 nm in the range of 2–5 nm. In addition, the disk-like configuration of GQDs further facilitated the formation of more mature nanostructures. AFM height images and height profiles of GQD and CQD precursors are represented in Fig. S2. The respective SAED patterns of GQDs and CQDs are also illustrated in the inset panels of Fig. 1(a) and (b), respectively, which are fingerprints of somehow crystalline natures.

By using the PBDT-DTNT chains, the patterned configurations were developed. The mentioned nanostructures composed of GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain and CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain are displayed in Fig. 2 (a)–(d), respectively. The SAED patterns and histograms of lateral size/thickness distributions are represented in different parts of Fig. 2. The PBDT-DTNT conjugated chains for having a complicated configuration were capable of forming only some patterns onto either GQD or CQD nanoparticles. The shorter PBDT-DTNT chains acted better and developed the greater patterns. The lateral sizes of patterned nanostructures of GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (Fig. 2(a, bottom)), GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (Fig. 2(b, bottom)), CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (Fig. 2(c, bottom)) and CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (Fig. 2(d, bottom)) were significantly similar to those of pristine QDs (Fig. 1(a) and (b)). This phenomenon demonstrated that the PBDT-DTNT chains did not completely embrace the QDs and only patterned their surfaces. However, the thicknesses increased with respect to the pristine QDs (Fig. 2 versus Fig. 1). The thickness distributions of GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain and CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain patterned nano-hybrids ranged in 2–6 nm and peaked at 5 nm, but with various percentages. AFM height images and height profiles of GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain and CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain nanostructures are depicted in Fig. S3. By enhancing the length of PBDT-DTNT chains, the thickness histograms were moved towards the lower thicknesses. Therefore, the arrangement of PBDT-DTNT shorter chains onto the GQDs and CQDs was more facile compared to their longer chains. Beside the prisms of QDs, the arc-like patterns were detected in the SAED plots of the GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain and CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain nanostructures, which were correlated with partially ordering of PBDT-DTNT conductive chains (Fig. 2(a)–(d)).

The schematic structures of GQD and CQD nanoparticles patterned by the PBDT-DTNT long and short chains are shown in Fig. 3 (a)–(d). The shorter PBDT-DTNT chains resulted in the larger and more connected patterns onto the carbonic QDs. Despite the fact that there was no conspicuous difference between the GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, patterned GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain and patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain nanostructures, their respective photovoltaic dada differed from each other. More details will be rendered in upcoming sections.

Different supramolecules on the basis of GQD and CQD nanoparticles and the short and long P3HT chains have been also designed to be applied in the upcoming photovoltaic cells. The crystallization of P3HT short chains in the presence of GQD and CQD nanoparticles reflected the crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules, respectively. TEM images, SAED patterns, and the lateral size/thickness histograms of the mentioned nano-hybrids are displayed in Fig. 4 (a) and (c), respectively. The feet of crabs were composed of the face-on and edge-on oriented P3HT fibrillar crystals. The SAED patterns with (100) and (002) prisms in the directions of hexyl side chains and main backbone longitude, respectively, manifested a face-on orientation for the P3HT nanofibrils, fabricated by the P3HT short chains. Likewise, the appearance of (020) and (002) growth planes in the π–π stacking and backbone longitude directions approved an edge-on orientation for the P3HT nanofibrils.

The disk-like GQDs as the seeding agents acted better than the spherical CQDs in the crystallization of P3HT short chains. The development of mature crab-like supramolecules with the coarser and longer P3HT feet approved this claim. The crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain nanostructures were common in the face-on and edge-on orientations. The overall lateral sizes of crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules ranged in 10–25 nm and 5–25 nm with the peaks of 15 and 10 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the thicknesses of both crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules ranged in 2–6 nm and peaked at 4 nm. However, the portion of larger thicknesses was higher in the crab-like GQD/P3HT nanostructure (Fig. 4(a) versus Fig. 4(c)). AFM height images and height profiles of crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain supramolecules are illustrated in Fig. S4.

By increasing the length of P3HT chains, the pellet-like configurations were developed instead of the crab/semicrab-like nanostructures. Actually the longer P3HT chains did not have opportunity to be further folded and be inserted into the nanofibrillar structures. Instead, these long P3HT chains were arranged onto the surface of GQDs and CQDs to completely cover them. The typical pellet-like GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT supramolecules are illustrated in Fig. 4(b) and (d), accompanied by the corresponding SAED patterns and lateral size/thickness histograms.

By regarding the SAED patterns of Fig. 4(b) and (d) for the GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT pellet-like nanostructures, respectively, the face-on and edge-on orientations were detected, which were weaker with respect to the crab/semicrab-like supramolecules. The histograms of lateral size and thickness distributions were moved towards the larger values in the pellet-like nanostructures (Fig. 4(b) and (d)) with respect to the pristine QDs (Fig. 1). The coarser structures of pellet-like supramolecules than those of corresponding QDs approved that the P3HT long chains have completely covered their seeds during the crystallization procedures. The lateral sizes of both GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT pellet-like nanostructures ranged in 2–6 nm and also peaked at 4 nm. Furthermore, the thicknesses of GQD- and CQD-based pellet-like nano-hybrids were distributed in the range of 3–6 nm with the maximum values of 5 nm (Fig. 4(b) and (d)). The type of seed, i.e., GQD or CQD, did not affect the shape, size, and configuration of designed nanostructures by using the P3HT long chains.

The schemes of crab-like, semicrab-like, and pellet-like nano-hybrids are depicted in Fig. 5 (a)–(d). As demonstrated by the SAED patterns of TEM analyses, all these nanostructures possessed both edge-on and face-on orientations. The patterned GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (Fig. 3(a)), patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (Fig. 3(b)), patterned GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (Fig. 3(c)), patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain (Fig. 3(d)), crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (Fig. 5(a)), semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain nanostructures (Fig. 5(b)), pellet-like GQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures (Fig. 5(c)) and pellet-like CQD/P3HT long chain (Fig. 5(d)) supramolecules were used as the morphology modifier, donor, and acceptor precursors in various active layers.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the structure of a typical photovoltaic cell fabricated in this work, which was composed of the ITO (anode), PEDOT:PSS (anodic buffer layer), active layer, LiF (cathodic buffer layer) and Al (cathode). The schemes of modifying supramolecules and the chemical structures of the PBDT-DTNT and PC71BM are also displayed in the left panel of Fig. 6(a). The energy levels of various nanostructures, used in the photovoltaics, are also displayed in Fig. 6(b). The lower bandgaps led to better photovoltaic results, which will be discussed in upcoming sections.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of prepared thin films were recorded on a Lambda 750 UV–vis spectrometer using the ultimate solutions. Fig. S5 depicts UV–vis spectra of different samples including the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM basic sample and those modified by the CQD, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, CQD/P3HT short chain, CQD/P3HT long chain, GQD, GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, GQD/P3HT short chain and GQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures. The GQD-based nanostructures acted better than the correlated CQD-based ones, approved by more intensified and red-shifted peaks in UV–vis spectra. Moreover, the P3HT-based supramolecules, because of having more ordered structures, further improved the absorbance properties from both peak position and intensity perspectives.

The J–V curves of ternary and binary photovoltaic cells are represented in Fig. 7 (a)–(c). The data details of short circuit current density (Jsc), open circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF), PCE, hole mobility (μh) and electron mobility (μe) are reported in Table S1. Experimental dark current densities (JD), recorded in electron/hole-only instruments, are reported in Fig. S6. The applied voltage (V) was corrected for built-in voltage (VBI) and voltage drop (VRS) against the ITO/PEDOT:PSS series resistance (30 Ω). In addition, the bar graphs of Jsc (Fig. 8 (a)), FF (Fig. 8(b)), Voc (Fig. 8(c)) and PCE (Fig. 8(d)) features are illustrated in different parts of Fig. 8 for the ternary PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:supramolecule as well as the binary PBDT-DTNT:supramolecule and PC71BM:supramolecule systems for a better comparison. The pristine PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM solar cells resulted in the Jsc, FF, Voc, μh, μe and PCE of 9.64 mA cm–2, 58.11%, 0.62 V, 3.8 × 10–4 cm2 V–1 s–1, 1.2 × 10–3 cm2 V–1 s–1 and 3.47%, respectively. The insertion of primary CQD and GQD nanoparticles in these photoactive layers did not considerably affect the cell features. The data couples of (10.02 and 10.67 mA cm–2), (58.92 and 59.49%), (0.62 and 0.62 V), (7.4 × 10–4 and 8.1 × 10–4 cm2 V–1 s–1), (6.5 × 10–3 and 7.3 × 10–3 cm2 V–1 s–1) and (3.66 and 3.93%) were obtained for the CQD- and GQD-based devices, respectively.

The use of pre-designed patterned CQD/PBDT-DTNT and GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain nano-hybrids in the active layers as the morphology modifier promoted the photovoltaic characteristics up to (11.66 and 11.89 mA cm–2), (60.43 and 62.02%), (0.65 and 0.65 V), (9.7 × 10–4 and 1.8 × 10–3 cm2 V–1 s–1), (8.9 × 10–3 and 2.9 × 10–2 cm2 V–1 s–1) and, eventually, (4.58 and 4.79%). Through enhancing the molecular weight of PBDT-DTNT chains from 7500 to 35 000 g/mol, their coverage onto the QDs decreased and parallelly, the quality of supramolecules as the morphology modifier plummeted. The photovoltaic data of (10.51 mA cm–2, 59.45%, 0.62 V and 3.87%) and (10.75 mA cm–2, 61.10%, 0.63 V and 4.14%) were recorded in the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM systems modified with the CQD/PBDT-DTNT and GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, respectively.

In another empirical work, the regioregular P3HT-based nano-hybrids were developed and applied in the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM ternary photoactive layers. The results demonstrated that the P3HT-based supramolecules acted conspicuously better than the PBDT-DTNT ones as the third component in the ternary systems. Among them, the crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain supramolecules were the best and reflected the largest PCE values obtained in this research. The Jsc, FF, Voc, μh and μe of (13.35 and 14.63 mA cm–2), (66.79 and 68.71%), (0.70 and 0.71 V), (8.2 × 10–3 and 9.0 × 10–3 cm2 V–1 s–1) and (9.4 × 10–2 and 9.8 × 10–2 cm2 V–1 s–1) for the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain systems led to the PCE values of 6.24 and 7.14%, respectively. By comparing the SAED patterns recorded for the PBDT-DTNT- and P3HT-based supramolecules, it could be elicited that the latter ones were composed of the single crystalline nanostructures having the sharp growth prisms of (100), (020), and (002) (Fig. 4 versus Fig. 2). Furthermore, the nanofibrillar P3HT crystals developed in the crab/semicrab-like supramolecules were significantly more ordered than the PBDT-DTNT patches on the QDs. Therefore, the higher efficacies in the photovoltaics modified by the P3HT-based nano-hybrids could be correlated with highly crystallizable nature of regioregular P3HTs.

In contrast, as above-mentioned, the longer P3HT chains were not as successful as their short counterparts in constructing the fibrillar crystals. Instead, the P3HT long chains fully covered the QD nanoparticles and reflected the pellet-like supramolecules, as displayed in Fig. 4(b) and (d). The arranged P3HT chains onto both GQD and CQD nanoparticles also fabricated the single crystalline nanostructures, but not as perfect as those detected in the crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules. The pellet-like nano-hybrids also considerably promoted the photovoltaic characteristics in the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM solar cells. The data couples of (12.08 and 13.38 mA cm–2), (62.12 and 66.85%), (0.67 and 0.69 V), (2.3 × 10–3 and 7.4 × 10–3 cm2 V–1 s–1), (3.5 × 10–2 and 8.6 × 10–2 cm2 V–1 s–1) and (5.03 and 6.17%) were acquired for the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT long chain and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT long chain devices, respectively. Despite the fact that the pellet-like supramolecules possessing the long P3HT chains were not as proper as the crab/semicrab-like nano-hybrids having the short P3HT chains, they acted better than all other modifiers discussed up to now in the current work.

In addition to the ternary photovoltaic cells, the binary systems were fabricated using the QDs and their associated supramolecules as donor and acceptor agents. In the latter devices, the pristine PBDT-DTNT:CQD and PBDT-DTNT:GQD solar cells resulted in (7.42 mA cm–2, 40.13%, 0.50 V and 1.49%) and (8.14 mA cm–2, 41.29%, 0.51 V and 1.71%), respectively. The J–V curves of these types of photovoltaics are represented in Fig. 7(b). The data details are also tabulated in Table S1. The bar graphs of Jsc, FF, Voc and PCE are also drawn in Fig. 8(a)–(d), respectively, for further comparison with the other ternary and binary systems. Exactly similar to the ternary devices, the crab/semicrab-like and pellet-like nano-hybrids, which were fabricated on the basis of the P3HT, acted better than the patterned nanostructures prepared by the PBDT-DTNT chains. The best results were recorded for the PBDT-DTNT:GQD/P3HT short chain (11.25 mA cm–2, 45.73%, 0.57 V, 5.2 × 10–6 cm2 V–1 s–1, 7.1 × 10–5 cm2 V–1 s–1 and 2.93%) and the PBDT-DTNT:CQD/P3HT short chain (10.77 mA cm–2, 45.12%, 0.55 V, 1.6 × 10–6 cm2 V–1 s–1, 3.0 × 10–5 cm2 V–1 s–1 and 2.67%) solar cells. The PBDT-DTNT based counterparts were not as prosperous as the discussed systems and, the PCE values of 2.13% (9.38 mA cm–2, 42.91%, and 0.53 V) and 1.85% (8.56 mA cm–2, 41.67%, and 0.52 V) were detected for the GQD- and CQD-based nanostructures, respectively. The longer polymer chains, either P3HT or PBDT-DTNT, decreased the cell characteristics because of their lower-quality nano-hybrids (Figs. 2 and 4). The performances of 2.67, 2.36, 1.90 and 1.63% were achieved in the devices based on the PBDT-DTNT donor as well as the acceptors of GQD/P3HT long chain, CQD/P3HT long chain, GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain and CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, respectively. The data details are also reported in Table S1.

In the second type of binary photovoltaic cells, the QDs and their nano-hybrids were utilized as the electron donor. The pure GQD and CQD nanoparticles were not successful in the donor role and the obtained data were not acceptable, i.e., (1.03 mA cm–2, 8.21%, 0.21 V and 0.02%) for the PC71BM:CQD and (1.09 mA cm–2, 9.38%, 0.26 V and 0.03%) for the PC71BM:GQD devices. The P3HT and PBDT-DTNT chains in the pre-designed nanostructures to some extent improved the cell function. Among them, the crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (3.01 mA cm–2, 20.12%, 0.37 V, 9.0 × 10–12 cm2 V–1 s–1, 9.8 × 10–10 cm2 V–1 s–1 and 0.22%), the semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain (2.65 mA cm–2, 17.71%, 0.34 V, 6.3 × 10–12 cm2 V–1 s–1, 7.2 × 10–10 cm2 V–1 s–1 and 0.16%), and the pellet-like GQD/P3HT long chain (2.70 mA cm–2, 16.67%, 0.35 V, 6.8 × 10–12 cm2 V–1 s–1, 8.1 × 10–10 cm2 V–1 s–1 and 0.16%) nano-hybrids were the best. The photovoltaic properties of other cells are also reported in Table S1. The J–V curves and Jsc, FF, Voc and PCE bar graphs are represented in Figs. 7(c) and 8 (a)–(d).

The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra were recorded on a chamber equipped with a Xe lamp (200 W), a monochromator (CVI), a calibrated photodetector (Newport 818), a lens module, and an electrometer (Keithley 237). According to Fig. 9 , the best peak values of 88–90% at 654 nm and 85–86% at 635 nm were observed for the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT short chain systems, respectively. The GQD/P3HT long chain (82–84% at 634 nm), CQD/P3HT long chain (78–80% at 615 nm), GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (75–76% at 603 nm) and CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain (68–70% at 600 nm) supramolecules also promoted the EQE. The peak positions and intensities in the EQE of binary systems were significantly less than those detected in the associated ternary photoactive layers (Fig. 9(b) and (c) versus Fig. 9(a)). The best results were recorded for the PBDT-DTNT:GQD/P3HT short chain devices (44–46% at 600 nm) in Fig. 9(b) and for the PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain systems (12–13% at 592 nm) in Fig. 9(c). The Jsc values calculated from the EQE experiments, which were somehow less than those obtained from the J–V curves, are also reported in Table S1. The AM 1.5 employed in EQE systems was standard, whereas the simulation was performed with Xenon lamp in a solar simulator. Moreover, all photons reaching a photovoltaic device were not transferred into the active layer for conversion.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data for all devices were fitted in a simple circuit with the charge transfer resistance (Rtr), as displayed in the insets of Fig. 10 (a)–(c). Rtr is originated from the interfacial charge transport process, which is illustrated by semicircle radii of Nyquist plots. The smaller Rtr exhibits the less charge transport obstruction, which is conducive to charge transfer, leading to the larger charge currents and extractions [92]. In addition, Rs is also defined as series resistance, resulting from the electrodes and bulk resistance. The Nyquist plots were drawn in dark condition as Z′ (real cell impedance) versus –Z″ (imaginary cell impedance). Fig. 11 also represents the bar graphs of Rtr variances for all ternary and binary systems, whose data details are reported in Table S1. As represented in Fig. 10(a) and Table S1, the Rtr values of the ternary devices based on the CQD, CQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, CQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, CQD/P3HT short chain, CQD/P3HT long chain, GQD, GQD/PBDT-DTNT short chain, GQD/PBDT-DTNT long chain, GQD/P3HT short chain, GQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures were 680, 597, 651, 447, 561, 645, 583, 626, 287 and 456 Ω cm2, respectively. The thin films modified by the crab/semicrab-like nanostructures led to the faster electron transport and thus the lower carrier recombination with respect to the other solar cells. On the other side, the binary photovoltaics were associated with very larger Rtr values, which was consistent with the above-discussed data. The largest Rtr values were recorded for the binary PC71BM:CQD (1029 Ω cm2) and PC71BM:GQD (1026 Ω cm2) devices (Fig. 10(c) and Table S1). Among the binary photovoltaics, the most acceptable Rtr data were recorded for the PBDT-DTNT:GQD/P3HT short chain (731 Ω cm2), PBDT-DTNT:GQD/P3HT long chain (758 Ω cm2), and PBDT-DTNT:CQD/P3HT short chain (760 Ω cm2) supramolecules (Figs. 10(b), 11, and Table S1).

Although by adopting the PBDT-DTNT chains, the patterned nano-hybrids were developed in all cases, the P3HTs led to different crab-like, semicrab-like, and pellet-like configurations. The designed nanostructures were used in the roles of modifier, donor, and acceptor constituents in the ternary and binary photoactive layers. The use of pre-designed QD/PBDT-DTNT short chain nano-hybrids as morphology modifier promoted the PCE up to 4.79%. The P3HT-based supramolecules acted significantly better than the PBDT-DTNT ones and the highest efficacies of 7.14 and 6.24% were detected for the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT short chain devices, respectively. Despite the fact that the pellet-like supramolecules possessing the long P3HT chains were not as proper as the crab/semicrab-like nano-hybrids having the short P3HT chains, they acted better than all other modifiers and the PCE values of 5.03 and 6.17% were recorded for the PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT long chain and PBDT-DTNT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT long chain photovoltaics, respectively. Besides the ternary photovoltaics, the binary PBDT-DTNT:supramolecule and PC71BM:supramolecule systems were constructed for a better comparison. Among them, the PBDT-DTNT:GQD/P3HT short chain with a performance of 2.93% and the PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain with an efficacy of 0.22% were the best prepared binary solar cells.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2021.04.051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Polym. Sci. B Polym. Phys.
